questionText: Prove that $n^3 + 2n$ is divisible by $3$ for every integer $n$.

steps.description: Rewrite to expose a familiar expression.
steps.explanation: Splitting $2n$ as $-n+3n$ is a clever regrouping: it turns part of the expression into $n^3-n$, which factors into consecutive integers.
Using $n^3-n=n(n^2-1)=(n-1)n(n+1)$ produces three consecutive integers, a structure that guarantees a multiple of $3$.
Combining the two parts gives a sum of a triple product and $3n$; we now show each piece is divisible by $3$.

steps.description: Divisibility of the product — set up cases.
steps.explanation: Every integer leaves remainder $0$, $1$ or $2$ modulo $3$; checking these exhaustive cases settles the product.
If $n$ is a multiple of $3$, the middle factor $n$ makes the product divisible by $3$.
If $n\equiv1$, then $n-1\equiv0$, so the factor $n-1$ is divisible by $3$.
If $n\equiv2$, then $n+1\equiv0$, so the factor $n+1$ is divisible by $3$. In every case the product is a multiple of $3$.
The term $3n$ is plainly a multiple of $3$ for any integer $n$.
The sum of two multiples of $3$ is again a multiple of $3$, so the whole expression is divisible by $3$.
Both pieces are divisible by $3$, so $n^3+2n$ is divisible by $3$ for every integer $n$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $n^3+2n=(n-1)n(n+1)+3n$. The triple product of consecutive integers is divisible by $3$, and $3n$ is too, so their sum is divisible by $3$. $\blacksquare$
